diagrams.title: Abstraction Layers Pyramid
diagrams.description: A layered pyramid diagram showing how abstraction works in software engineering, with each level hiding complexity from the layers above. The bottom layer represents raw data, middle layers represent implementation details, and the top represents the user interface - only showing what's necessary.
diagrams.purpose: Explains the concept of abstraction - how complex systems hide implementation details behind simple interfaces, allowing developers to work at different levels of detail without needing to understand everything below.

diagrams.title: Modular Architecture
diagrams.description: A diagram showing a software system decomposed into independent modules, each with its own responsibility and well-defined interface. Modules communicate only through these interfaces, demonstrating the modularity principle.
elements: Internal implementation details (hidden within each module)
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates modularity - how decomposing a system into independent, focused modules improves maintainability, reusability, and allows teams to work on different modules simultaneously.

diagrams.title: Independence Through Loose Coupling
diagrams.description: A diagram showing independent components connected through abstraction layers/contracts, demonstrating how changes in one component do not affect others. Shows both tight coupling (direct dependencies) and loose coupling (abstract interfaces) for comparison.
elements: Direct dependency arrows (showing tight coupling - crossed out)
diagrams.purpose: Explains independence - how loose coupling through well-defined interfaces allows components to be developed, tested, and modified independently, improving flexibility and reducing the impact of changes.
